---
title: "R Notebook"
output: html_notebook
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

# Biostatistics Project Report : Transcriptome Analyses

Clears Environment
```{r}
rm(list = ls())
```

sets the working directory
```{r}
setwd("F:/semesters/sem8/biostatistics/transcriptome-data-analysis")
getwd()
```
imports Infect file and plots the barplot.
```{r}

infect <- read.table(
  file = "infect.txt",
  header = FALSE,
  sep = "\t",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

colnames(infect) <- c("SRR", "Infectivity", "CellLine")
infect$Infectivity <- as.numeric(infect$Infectivity)

############################################
## CONDITIONAL COLORING (FIXED)
############################################

infect$color <- ifelse(
  infect$Infectivity < 4, "red3",
  ifelse(infect$Infectivity < 30, "seagreen3", "steelblue")
)

############################################
## SORT
############################################

infect_sorted <- infect[order(infect$Infectivity), ]

############################################
## PLOT
############################################

par(mar = c(4, 10, 2, 1))

barplot(
  infect_sorted$Infectivity,
  horiz = TRUE,
  names.arg = infect_sorted$CellLine,
  las = 1,
  col = infect_sorted$color,
  border = NA,
  xlab = "Nef+/Nef- infectivity ratio",
  xlim = c(0, max(infect_sorted$Infectivity) * 1.1),
  cex.names = 0.8
)
```
**Figure 1:** Bar plot

*imports the files of transcriptome of cell lines*

```{r}

gene_id <- "ENSG00000164300"
expr_folder <- "F:/semesters/sem8/biostatistics/transcriptome-data-analysis/gene data"

############################################
## -------- PREP INFECT (NO READING) --------
############################################

# Just ensure columns are correct
colnames(infect) <- c("SRR", "Infectivity", "CellLine")
infect$Infectivity <- as.numeric(infect$Infectivity)

############################################
## -------- STEP 1: MAP SRR IDs --------
############################################

file_to_cellline <- c(
  "SRR2166626" = "HT1080",
  "SRR2166634" = "CEM X 174",
  "SRR2166632" = "IMR90",
  "SRR2166624" = "MT4",
  "SRR2166629" = "DAUDI",
  "SRR2166635" = "WI38",
  "SRR2166630" = "C8166",
  "SRR2166627" = "RAJI",
  "SRR2166625" = "HSB2",
  "SRR2166628" = "CEM SS",
  "SRR2166633" = "CEM A 301",
  "SRR2166631" = "RAMOS",
  "SRR2166637" = "bl41",
  "SRR2166636" = "JURKAT E6.1",
  "SRR2166638" = "Jurkat tag"
)

############################################
## -------- STEP 2: READ EXPRESSION --------
############################################

files <- list.files(expr_folder, full.names = TRUE)

read_expr <- function(file) {
  df <- read.table(file, header = TRUE, sep = "\t", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  df <- df[, 1:2]

  srr <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(file))
  cellline <- file_to_cellline[srr]

  colnames(df) <- c("GeneID", cellline)
  df
}

expr_list <- lapply(files, read_expr)

expr_merged <- Reduce(
  function(x, y) merge(x, y, by = "GeneID", all = TRUE),
  expr_list
)

############################################
## -------- STEP 3: EXTRACT SERINC5 --------
############################################

serinc5 <- expr_merged[expr_merged$GeneID == gene_id, -1]

serinc5 <- as.data.frame(t(serinc5))
serinc5$CellLine <- rownames(serinc5)
rownames(serinc5) <- NULL

colnames(serinc5) <- c("Expression", "CellLine")
serinc5$Expression <- as.numeric(serinc5$Expression)

############################################
## -------- STEP 4: MERGE --------
############################################

data <- merge(infect, serinc5, by = "CellLine")
data <- data[complete.cases(data[, c("Infectivity", "Expression")]), ]

cat("Number of matched samples:", nrow(data), "\n")

############################################
## -------- STEP 5: CONDITIONAL COLORS --------
############################################
## (using your new logic instead of manual mapping)

data$color <- ifelse(
  data$Infectivity < 4, "red3",
  ifelse(data$Infectivity < 30, "seagreen3", "steelblue")
)

############################################
## -------- STEP 6: CORRELATION --------
############################################

ct <- cor.test(data$Expression, data$Infectivity)

r  <- round(ct$estimate, 3)
r2 <- round(r^2, 4)

print(ct)

############################################
## -------- STEP 7: SCATTER PLOT (UPDATED) --------
############################################

# Scale expression
data$Expression_scaled <- data$Expression / 100

par(mar = c(5, 5, 2, 1))

plot(
  data$Infectivity,          # X = infectivity
  data$Expression_scaled,    # Y = scaled expression
  pch = 21,
  bg  = data$color,
  col = "black",
  cex = 1.7,
  xlab = expression("Nef"^"+"*"/Nef"^"-"*" infectivity ratio"),
  ylab = "SERINC5 expression (scaled RPM)"
)

# Regression (IMPORTANT: update model)
abline(lm(Expression_scaled ~ Infectivity, data = data), lwd = 2)

# Annotation
text(
  x = min(data$Infectivity),
  y = max(data$Expression_scaled),
  labels = bquote(
    atop(
      italic(r) == .(r),
      atop(
        italic(R)^2 == .(r2),
        italic(P) < 0.001
      )
    )
  ),
  adj = c(0, 1),
  cex = 1
)
```
**Figure 2:** plot of the expression of serinc5 in various cell lines vs Nef+/Nef– infectivity ratio


## Statistical tests applied on SERINC5 expression levels for various 
### Pearson correlation
```{r}

cor.test(data$Expression, data$Infectivity, method = "pearson")
```
SERINC5 expression levels and INfectivity show a significant correlation of 89% (P***)

### linear regression
```{r}

model <- lm(Expression ~ Infectivity, data = data)
summary(model)

```
SERINC5 expression levels show a significant positive trend of with R2 value of 0.79 (P***)

### Group Comparison (t-test)
```{r}

data$Group <- ifelse(data$Infectivity < 4, "Low",
               ifelse(data$Infectivity < 30, "Medium", "High"))

t.test(Expression ~ Group, data = subset(data, Group %in% c("Low","High")))
##########Whether high vs low infectivity groups differ in SERINC5 expression

```
Although SERINC5 expression appears much higher in high-infectivity cell lines compared to low-infectivity ones, the difference was not statistically significant (Welch t-test, p = 0.104), likely due to small sample size and high variability.`

### ANOVA : comparing low Vs medium Vs High
```{r}

anova_model <- aov(Expression ~ Group, data = data)
summary(anova_model)

```
A one-way ANOVA revealed a highly significant difference in SERINC5 expression across infectivity groups (F = 60.55, p < 0.001), indicating that expression varies systematically with infectivity level.`

### TukeyHSD post-hoc test
```{r}

TukeyHSD(anova_model)

```
Tukey’s post-hoc analysis revealed significant differences between all infectivity groups. SERINC5 expression was highest in high-infectivity cell lines, intermediate in medium-infectivity lines, and lowest in low-infectivity lines (all pairwise comparisons p < 0.05), indicating a graded increase in expression with infectivity.

Earlier we saw:
    - t-test (Low vs High → not significant
    - ANOVA → significant
    - Tukey → ALL pairs significant

This is because Tukey uses pooled variance from ANOVA and is more powerful.

### Variance test F test:
```{r}

data_2 <- subset(data, Group %in% c("Low", "High"))
var.test(Expression ~ Group, data = data_2)
```
`Stable than Welch t-test on small samples.

There is a significant difference in variability of SERINC5 expression between the two groups.

This also explains earlier result:
t-test was not significant while ANOVA + Tukey were significant

Because:
    - Welch t-test accounts for unequal variance
    - High variance → reduces statistical power
    - So p-value becomes non-significant

### Normality check: Shapiro test`
```{r}

shapiro.test(data$Expression)
```
Data is normally distributed.

###False discovery rate (FDR) correction
False discovery rate (FDR) correction was applied to gene-wise correlation analyses to account for multiple testing. For targeted statistical tests (correlation, ANOVA, regression), correction was not required due to the limited number of hypotheses tested.

## QQ plots 
```{r}
par(mfrow = c(1,1))

qqnorm(data$Expression,
       main = "QQ Plot: SERINC5 Expression",
       pch = 19)

qqline(data$Expression, col = "red", lwd = 2)

```
**Figure 3:** QQ plot analysis revealed strong deviation from normality with right-skewed distribution of SERINC5 expression. This justifies the use of transformation or robust statistical methods.


```{r}
qqnorm(data$Infectivity,
       main = "QQ Plot: Infectivity",
       pch = 19)

qqline(data$Infectivity, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

```
**Figure 4:** Infectivity values deviate from normality, showing a right-skewed distribution with a heavy upper tail and clustering of low values.

```{r}
par(mfrow = c(1,3))

groups <- unique(data$Group)

for (g in groups) {
  subset_data <- data$Expression[data$Group == g]

  qqnorm(subset_data,
         main = paste("QQ:", g),
         pch = 19)

  qqline(subset_data, col = "red", lwd = 2)
}

```
**Figure 5:** QQ plots indicate that expression values deviate from normality across groups, particularly in the low and medium infectivity groups, while the high group has insufficient sample size to assess distribution.



```{r}
############################################
## CONTINUATION: GENE-WISE CORRELATION
############################################

############################################
## -------- STEP 1: READ & MERGE EXPRESSION --------
############################################

# Reuse expr_folder already defined

files <- list.files(expr_folder, full.names = TRUE)

read_expr <- function(file) {
  df <- read.table(file, header = TRUE, sep = "\t", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  df <- df[, 1:2]

  srr <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(file))
  cellline <- file_to_cellline[srr]

  colnames(df) <- c("GeneID", cellline)
  df
}

expr_list <- lapply(files, read_expr)

expr_merged <- Reduce(
  function(x, y) merge(x, y, by = "GeneID", all = TRUE),
  expr_list
)

############################################
## -------- STEP 2: ALIGN WITH INFECTIVITY --------
############################################

cell_order <- infect$CellLine

# Ensure all names exist
common_cells <- intersect(cell_order, colnames(expr_merged))

expr_mat <- expr_merged[, common_cells]

# Match infect order
infect_sub <- infect[infect$CellLine %in% common_cells, ]
expr_mat <- expr_mat[, infect_sub$CellLine]

expr_mat <- apply(expr_mat, 2, as.numeric)

############################################
## -------- STEP 3: GENE-WISE CORRELATION --------
############################################

cor_results <- apply(expr_mat, 1, function(gene_expr) {

  valid <- is.finite(gene_expr) & is.finite(infect_sub$Infectivity)

  if (sum(valid) < 3) {
    return(c(r = NA, R2 = NA, p = NA))
  }

  ct <- cor.test(
    gene_expr[valid],
    infect_sub$Infectivity[valid],
    method = "pearson"
  )

  c(
    r  = unname(ct$estimate),
    R2 = unname(ct$estimate)^2,
    p  = ct$p.value
  )
})

############################################
## -------- STEP 4: RESULTS TABLE --------
############################################

cor_df <- data.frame(
  GeneID = expr_merged$GeneID,
  t(cor_results),
  row.names = NULL
)

cor_df$r  <- as.numeric(cor_df$r)
cor_df$R2 <- as.numeric(cor_df$R2)
cor_df$p  <- as.numeric(cor_df$p)

############################################
## -------- STEP 5: MULTIPLE TEST CORRECTION --------
############################################

cor_df$FDR <- p.adjust(cor_df$p, method = "BH")

############################################
## -------- STEP 6: SORT --------
############################################

cor_df <- cor_df[order(-abs(cor_df$r)), ]

############################################
## -------- OUTPUT --------
############################################

head(cor_df)

# Save results (optional)
write.csv(cor_df, "gene_correlation_results.csv", row.names = FALSE)

cor_df[cor_df$GeneID == "ENSG00000164300", ]
```
Genome-wide correlation analysis identified genes whose expression correlates with infectivity, with SERINC5 ranking among the top candidates, supporting its functional role. The correlations, R2, p, and FDR for all the genes is in a csv file.  


### Volcano plot
```{r}
############################################
## CLEAN VOLCANO PLOT (SMALL-n CORRELATIONS)
############################################

## thresholds
r_cutoff   <- 0.6
fdr_cutoff <- 0.05
top_n      <- 10   # number of genes to label

## prepare data
cor_df$negLogFDR <- -log10(cor_df$FDR)

cor_df$Group <- "Not significant"
cor_df$Group[cor_df$FDR < fdr_cutoff & cor_df$r >  r_cutoff] <- "Positive"
cor_df$Group[cor_df$FDR < fdr_cutoff & cor_df$r < -r_cutoff] <- "Negative"

cols <- c(
  "Positive"        = "firebrick3",
  "Negative"        = "steelblue",
  "Not significant" = "grey70"
)

## plot
par(mar = c(5, 5, 2, 1))

plot(
  cor_df$r,
  cor_df$negLogFDR,
  pch = 16,
  col = cols[cor_df$Group],
  cex = 0.7,
  xlab = "Pearson correlation (r)",
  ylab = expression(-log[10]("FDR")),
  main = "Gene expression vs Infectivity"
)

## threshold lines
abline(v = c(-r_cutoff, r_cutoff), lty = 2)
abline(h = -log10(fdr_cutoff), lty = 2)

## legend
legend(
  "topright",
  legend = names(cols),
  col = cols,
  pch = 16,
  bty = "n"
)

## label only top genes
top_hits <- cor_df[cor_df$Group != "Not significant", ]
top_hits <- top_hits[order(-abs(top_hits$r)), ][1:min(top_n, nrow(top_hits)), ]

if (nrow(top_hits) > 0) {
  text(
    top_hits$r,
    top_hits$negLogFDR,
    labels = top_hits$GeneID,
    pos = 3,
    cex = 0.7
  )
}

```
**Figure 6:** Volcano plot between Gene expression and Infectivity

### CORRELATION vs MEAN EXPRESSION
```{r}
############################################
## CORRELATION vs MEAN EXPRESSION
############################################

cor_df$MeanExpr <- rowMeans(expr_mat, na.rm = TRUE)

plot(
  cor_df$r,
  log2(cor_df$MeanExpr + 1),
  pch = 16,
  col = cols[cor_df$Group],
  cex = 0.7,
  xlab = "Pearson correlation (r)",
  ylab = expression(log[2]("Mean expression (RPM + 1)")),
  main = "Correlation vs expression abundance"
)

abline(v = c(-r_cutoff, r_cutoff), lty = 2)

legend(
  "bottomright",
  legend = names(cols),
  col = cols,
  pch = 16,
  bty = "n"
)

```
**Figure 7:** correlation Vs expression-Abudance plot: Are strong correlations driven by low-expression noise or real biology? Many of these genes are moderately to highly expressed
This suggests they are stable cellular programs, not noise

### BOXPLOT WITH TOP 10 GENE NAMES ONLY
```{r}

############################################
## BOXPLOT WITH TOP 10 GENE NAMES ONLY
############################################
library("biomaRt")
# Top 10 genes
top_n <- 10
top_genes <- cor_df$GeneID[1:top_n]

# Connect to Ensembl (use mirror to avoid timeout)
mart <- useEnsembl(
  biomart = "genes",
  dataset = "hsapiens_gene_ensembl",
  mirror = "asia"
)

# Query ONLY top 10 genes
gene_map <- getBM(
  attributes = c("ensembl_gene_id", "hgnc_symbol"),
  filters = "ensembl_gene_id",
  values = top_genes,
  mart = mart
)

# Create lookup
gene_lookup <- setNames(gene_map$hgnc_symbol, gene_map$ensembl_gene_id)

# Replace missing names
top_gene_names <- gene_lookup[top_genes]
top_gene_names[is.na(top_gene_names) | top_gene_names == ""] <- top_genes

############################################
## EXPRESSION EXTRACTION
############################################

expr_top <- expr_merged[expr_merged$GeneID %in% top_genes, ]

expr_top_mat <- expr_top[, -1]
expr_top_mat <- apply(expr_top_mat, 2, as.numeric)

# Log transform
expr_top_mat <- log2(expr_top_mat + 1)

# Transpose
expr_top_t <- as.data.frame(t(expr_top_mat))

# Apply gene names
colnames(expr_top_t) <- top_gene_names

############################################
## PLOT
############################################

par(mar = c(8, 4, 2, 1))

boxplot(
  expr_top_t,
  las = 2,
  col = "grey85",
  border = "grey40",
  ylab = "Expression (log2)",
  main = "Top 10 Correlated Genes"
)
```
**Figure 8:** Box plot of 10 most correlated genes


### HEATMAP WITH GENE NAMES (biomaRt, TOP 10 ONLY)
```{r}
############################################
## HEATMAP WITH GENE NAMES (biomaRt, TOP 10 ONLY)
############################################

# Connect to Ensembl
mart <- useMart(
  biomart = "ensembl",
  dataset = "hsapiens_gene_ensembl"
)

gene_map <- getBM(
  attributes = c("ensembl_gene_id", "hgnc_symbol"),
  filters = "ensembl_gene_id",
  values = top_genes,
  mart = mart
)

# Create lookup
gene_lookup <- setNames(gene_map$hgnc_symbol, gene_map$ensembl_gene_id)

# Replace missing names
top_gene_names <- gene_lookup[top_genes]
top_gene_names[is.na(top_gene_names) | top_gene_names == ""] <- top_genes

############################################
## HEATMAP DATA
############################################

# Reorder rows to match top_genes
expr_top <- expr_merged[match(top_genes, expr_merged$GeneID), ]

heat_mat <- expr_top[, -1]
heat_mat <- apply(heat_mat, 2, as.numeric)

# Log transform
heat_mat <- log2(heat_mat + 1)

# Use gene names instead of Ensembl IDs
rownames(heat_mat) <- top_gene_names

# Scale genes
heat_mat_scaled <- t(scale(t(heat_mat)))

############################################
## HEATMAP PLOT
############################################

heatmap(
  heat_mat_scaled,
  Rowv = NA,
  Colv = NA,
  col = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(50),
  margins = c(6, 8),
  main = "Top 10 Genes Heatmap"
)


```
**Figure 9:** Visualization of the top 10 correlated genes revealed distinct expression patterns across cell lines, with higher expression levels observed in high-infectivity samples, consistent with correlation analysis.

### Clustering
```{r}
dist_mat <- dist(t(expr_mat))
hc <- hclust(dist_mat)

plot(hc, main = "Hierarchical Clustering of Cell Lines")

```
**Figure 10:** Clustering: Based on Whole transcriptome

Infectivity is driven by: specific genes (like SERINC5) NOT the entire transcriptome
While infectivity groups show clear separation in targeted analyses, hierarchical clustering based on global gene expression reveals additional transcriptomic heterogeneity among cell lines.

Analysis     Uses                    Result
Bar plot     Infectivity only        clear groups
Scatter      SERINC5 vs infectivity  strong correlation
Clustering   ALL genes               mixed grouping


```{r}
top100 <- cor_df$GeneID[1:100]

expr_top <- expr_merged[expr_merged$GeneID %in% top100, -1]
expr_top <- apply(expr_top, 2, as.numeric)

dist_mat <- dist(t(expr_top))
hc <- hclust(dist_mat)

plot(hc, main = "Clustering using top correlated genes")

```
**Figure 11:** Dendrogram based on 100 most correlated genes
1. High group is very distinct

Strong separation
→ driven by top genes (like SERINC5)

2. Medium group is heterogeneous

Splits into subclusters
→ suggests different biological mechanisms

3. Low group is compact

Similar transcriptomes
→ consistently low expression of key genes

A subset of genes (top correlated genes) is sufficient to recover infectivity-based grouping of cell lines.

Hierarchical clustering based on top correlated genes revealed clear separation of high-infectivity cell lines from low-infectivity ones, with intermediate cell lines forming subclusters, indicating heterogeneity within the medium infectivity group.


### Principal Component Analysis
```{r}
# Remove low-variance genes
expr_mat_filtered <- expr_mat[
  apply(expr_mat, 1, function(x) var(x, na.rm = TRUE)) > 1e-5, ]

# Log transform (recommended)
expr_mat_filtered <- log2(expr_mat_filtered + 1)

# PCA
pca <- prcomp(t(expr_mat_filtered), scale. = TRUE)

# Variance explained
var_explained <- (pca$sdev^2) / sum(pca$sdev^2)
percent_var <- round(var_explained * 100, 2)

pc1_var <- percent_var[1]
pc2_var <- percent_var[2]

# Plot
plot(
  pca$x[,1], pca$x[,2],
  col = as.factor(data$Group),
  pch = 19,
  xlab = paste0("PC1 (", pc1_var, "%)"),
  ylab = paste0("PC2 (", pc2_var, "%)"),
  main = "PCA of Gene Expression"
)

legend("topright",
       legend = levels(as.factor(data$Group)),
       col = 1:length(unique(data$Group)),
       pch = 19)
```
**Figure 12:** PCA of all genes together
```{r}
############################################
## PCA WITH 3 PCs (TOP GENES)
############################################

top20 <- cor_df$GeneID[1:10]

# KEEP ORDER
expr_top <- expr_merged[match(top20, expr_merged$GeneID), -1]
expr_top <- apply(expr_top, 2, as.numeric)

# Log transform
expr_top <- log2(expr_top + 1)

# Remove zero variance
expr_top <- expr_top[apply(expr_top, 1, var) > 0, ]

# PCA
pca_top <- prcomp(t(expr_top), scale. = TRUE)

# Variance explained
var_explained_top <- (pca_top$sdev^2) / sum(pca_top$sdev^2)
percent_var_top <- round(var_explained_top * 100, 2)

pc1_var <- percent_var_top[1]
pc2_var <- percent_var_top[2]
pc3_var <- percent_var_top[3]

############################################
## -------- PAIRWISE PLOTS (BEST) --------
############################################

par(mfrow = c(1,3))

# PC1 vs PC2
plot(
  pca_top$x[,1], pca_top$x[,2],
  col = as.factor(data$Group),
  pch = 19,
  xlab = paste0("PC1 (", pc1_var, "%)"),
  ylab = paste0("PC2 (", pc2_var, "%)"),
  main = "PC1 vs PC2"
)

# PC1 vs PC3
plot(
  pca_top$x[,1], pca_top$x[,3],
  col = as.factor(data$Group),
  pch = 19,
  xlab = paste0("PC1 (", pc1_var, "%)"),
  ylab = paste0("PC3 (", pc3_var, "%)"),
  main = "PC1 vs PC3"
)

# PC2 vs PC3
plot(
  pca_top$x[,2], pca_top$x[,3],
  col = as.factor(data$Group),
  pch = 19,
  xlab = paste0("PC2 (", pc2_var, "%)"),
  ylab = paste0("PC3 (", pc3_var, "%)"),
  main = "PC2 vs PC3"
)

############################################
## -------- LEGEND --------
############################################

legend(
  "topright",
  legend = levels(as.factor(data$Group)),
  col = 1:length(unique(data$Group)),
  pch = 19
)
```
**Figure 13:** PCA for 20 most correlated genes

PCA of global gene expression does not show distinct clustering of infectivity groups, indicating that infectivity-related variation is driven by a subset of genes rather than overall transcriptomic differences.


### K- means clustering

```{r}
############################################
## K-MEANS CLUSTERING ON PCA
############################################

# Choose number of clusters (3 = Low/Medium/High)
k <- 3

# Run k-means on first 3 PCs
set.seed(123)
km <- kmeans(pca_top$x[,1:3], centers = k)

clusters <- as.factor(km$cluster)

############################################
## PLOT WITH CLUSTERS
############################################

par(mfrow = c(1,1))

plot(
  pca_top$x[,1], pca_top$x[,2],
  col = clusters,
  pch = 19,
  cex = 1.5,
  xlab = paste0("PC1 (", pc1_var, "%)"),
  ylab = paste0("PC2 (", pc2_var, "%)"),
  main = "PCA with K-means Clusters"
)

legend(
  "topright",
  legend = paste("Cluster", 1:k),
  col = 1:k,
  pch = 19
)
```
**Figure 14:** Clusters shown be different colours but not necessariry corresponting to the groups. 

data is gradient-like
Infectivity changes gradually
Not discrete groups

So PCA shows: overlap, spread, continuum

Your dataset has: Strong trend (gradient), Not perfectly separated clusters

While PCA suggests a continuous gradient of variation associated with infectivity, k-means clustering partitions this gradient into discrete groups, highlighting underlying structure but not strict separation.
